g.data-narration: Welcome! This lecture covers Relative and Absolute Pathnames and essential Directory Commands in Linux.
g.text: Relative and Absolute Pathnames - Directory Commands

g.data-narration: Pathnames are essential for navigating Linux file systems. They help locate files uniquely in the hierarchical directory structure.
g.text: Understanding pathnames is fundamental to working with Linux/Unix file systems.
Every file has a unique location identified by its path. Master both absolute
and relative paths for shell scripting, system admin, and file management.

g.data-narration: The Linux file system starts from root (/) and branches into standard directories like /home, /etc, /var, /usr, and /bin.
Absolute paths start from root (/) and provide exact file locations. They always begin with '/' and work from any directory.
g.text: Complete path starting from root (/). Always begins with "/".

g.data-narration: Relative paths specify file locations relative to your current directory. They don't start with '/' and depend on your CWD.
g.text: Path relative to current working directory. Never starts with "/".

g.data-narration: Linux provides special characters: dot (.) for current dir, double-dot (..) for parent, tilde (~) for home, hyphen (-) for previous.
The pwd command displays your current working directory. Use it to verify location before using relative paths.
g.text: Displays the absolute path of the current working directory.

g.data-narration: The cd command changes your current directory. Use absolute or relative paths, or special references like ~, -, and ..
g.text: Navigates to the specified directory path.

g.data-narration: The ls command lists directory contents. Use options like -l for details, -a for hidden files, and -R for recursive listing.
g.text: Displays information about files and directories.

g.data-narration: The mkdir command creates new directories. Use -p flag to create nested directories and -v for verbose output.
g.text: Creates new directories in the file system.

g.data-narration: The rmdir command removes empty directories. Use -p to remove empty parent directories. Cannot remove non-empty directories.
g.text: Removes empty directories from the file system.
rmdir only works on empty directories.

g.data-narration: Let's navigate a project structure using both absolute and relative paths to understand practical path resolution.
Absolute paths start with '/' and work anywhere; relative paths depend on current directory. Choose based on context and portability needs.
g.text: Starts with / | Always same | Works anywhere

g.data-narration: Remember these exam tips: absolute paths start with '/', use pwd to verify location, dot means current, double-dot parent, tilde home.
This concludes our lecture on pathnames and directory commands. Master pwd, cd, ls, mkdir, and rmdir for Linux file operations.
g.text: Commands Summary: pwd | cd | ls | mkdir | rmdir
